policy_statement: Agriculture adaptation strategy: strengthen CARI capacity for climate-smart agriculture, including establishing seed banks and improving soil management, crop diversification, and livestock breeds; and develop climate-resilient crops including drought-resistant and flood-tolerant varieties and gene banks.
mechanism_description: Strengthening agricultural research and developing climate-resilient seeds and practices (e.g., seed/gene banks, improved soil management, crop diversification, and hardier livestock/crop varieties) increases resilience to drought and flooding impacts.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (drought/flood/waterlogging) → improved soil & water management → ↓ exposure/sensitivity of crops & farms → ↓ climate-related yield loss and livelihood risk
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Changed human activity/system: Farmers adopt climate-smart agronomic practices such as integrated soil fertility management (e.g., organic amendments, balanced nutrients), water harvesting/storage, and soil & water conservation measures (e.g., mulching, contouring, erosion control, drainage/raised beds where relevant).  
2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Practices improve soil structure and organic matter, increase infiltration and soil water-holding capacity, reduce erosion/runoff, and (depending on design) improve drainage to avoid waterlogging; water harvesting increases water availability during dry spells.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Farms become less sensitive to drought and intense rainfall/flooding, sustaining productivity and reducing losses under climate variability and extremes (i.e., increased adaptive capacity and resilience).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is increasing agricultural system resilience to climate hazards (drought, flooding, waterlogging) by improving soil moisture retention/drainage and on-farm water availability.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism is explicitly framed as coping with aridity, waterlogging, and floods by improving soil fertility and water management—i.e., reducing climate-impact sensitivity and stabilizing yields.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While some practices can also reduce emissions or increase soil carbon (mitigation/nature-based co-benefits), the stated objective and largest effect across policies using this mechanism is resilience to climate hazards, not GHG reduction.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a specific policy under this mechanism were designed primarily to maximize carbon sequestration or reduce fertilizer-related N₂O, it could shift toward mitigation/NBS; however, the canonical mechanism here is “agricultural climate resilience,” which anchors the dominant pathway in adaptation.  
4) Confirm primary applies to ALL instances: Any policy that fits this mechanism (soil fertility + water harvesting + conservation to cope with extremes) primarily functions to reduce vulnerability to drought/flood/waterlogging, which is adaptation.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Co-benefits (soil carbon gains, reduced input use, methane/nitrous oxide changes) require policy- and practice-specific evidence and are therefore deferred to policy-level assessment.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some soil fertility and conservation measures can materially increase soil carbon or reduce N₂O (mitigation/Nature-Based Solutions co-benefits). Classification would only change if the policy’s primary intent and dominant quantified outcome is GHG mitigation rather than resilience to climate hazards.
key_indicators: “Adaptation strategies”; “Strengthen the Central Agricultural Research Institute (CARI) capacity”; “research development of climate smart agriculture”; “setting up of seed banks”; “soil management”; “crop diversification”; “improved livestock breeds”; “Develop climate-resilient crops”; “drought-resistant, flood-tolerant”; “establishing gene bank”
co_benefits: Food security and rural livelihoods improvement; biodiversity/agrobiodiversity conservation (via gene/seed banks); potential mitigation co-benefits from improved soil management (soil carbon)
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be read as an infrastructure/investment action (seed banks/gene banks as facilities), but the phrasing is predominantly capacity-building and strategy/research-oriented without clear budgeted capital works; classified as planning-strategy with enabling directness.
